title: First Interview
html:
The insurance company is in a building where the elevator is broken and the job listing didn't mention that. I find out on arrival and climb four flights with my blazer already sticking to my back. The office smells like old paper and the AC unit in the corner is running but not doing anything useful about the heat.

The interviewer is a woman in her forties who doesn't introduce herself and has my resume printed on paper, which is fine, except she's holding it at the angle of someone who's already decided. She asks me to walk her through my previous role.

I walk her through it. Four years, the client reports, the cross-departmental coordination, the software. I say cross-departmental coordination the way I wrote it on the resume, like it means something. She writes a note.

"Why data entry?" she asks.

"I need work," I say. "I'm good with volume and I don't make mistakes on repetitive tasks."

She writes another note. I can't tell if that was the right answer or not. She thanks me for my time, which is what you say when you've already made up your mind and are waiting for the other person to leave.

I climb back down four flights and ride home in traffic.

The ball of light is in my room. It has been there every time I've come back, same position, same dim glow. I change out of the blazer and check email. No response from the interview, which is expected. Two new application rejections from this morning. The acknowledgement of receipt from yesterday has not become anything.

I open a new document and start a list. Every application I've sent, the company, the role, the date, the status. Nineteen applications in four days. Three auto-rejections, two standard rejections, one acknowledgement, thirteen no response. That's the job market in a format I can look at without needing to feel anything about it.

The planet is still running. I open the Authority.

---

[Planet designation: Sphere 7-A]

[Status: Barren]

[Rotation period: 28 hours]

[Day 3 of 12: Water processing in progress.]

[Atmospheric development: 0.8% complete.]

[Terrain: Rocky. Surface water detected in low-lying regions.]

[Unclassified organisms: Status unknown. No Life Energy generation recorded.]

[Purchased organisms (x40): 31 surviving. Survival rate declining.]

[Life Energy generated: 0]

---

31 of 40 surviving. The unclassified organisms are whatever came in with the tap water, and the system doesn't know what to do with them. No Life Energy generation means they haven't reproduced or they're not alive in a way the system recognizes.

9 dead in 3 days. At that rate I'll have nothing left before the atmosphere finishes processing. I close the panel.

I heat up leftover rice and eat it standing in the kitchen. The ball follows me in. I've stopped being surprised by that.

There's a practical problem on the planet. 40 organisms on a barren rock was never a solution, it was a placeholder. The atmosphere needs to develop before anything survives long enough to reproduce, and it won't develop in time. The organisms will die, the tap water bacteria will freeze or desiccate, and I'll have a planet with water and no life. Then I need another loan, if a second loan is even available, or I need to generate Life Energy first, which I can't do with nothing alive.

I've been broke before. The logic of it is familiar. You need money to get money and the entry point is always the problem.

I sit at the desk and look at the application list. Nineteen applications. The interview today was bad. The woman had my resume at the wrong angle from the start.

The plant pot is still by the window. Bone dry. I take it to the sink and put some water in it. The soil absorbs it, dark spreading out from the center. I wait until the water stops absorbing and put the pot back.

I open the Authority and look at the planet panel for a long time.

The problem is volume. 40 organisms on a planet is nothing. Even if they all survive they can't generate enough Life Energy to register. The planet is too cold, too sparse. I need something that reproduces fast and survives hostile conditions.

I go back to the Shop and look at what 0 Life Energy buys.

Nothing from the Shop. That much is obvious.

But the tap water worked. It got there. Whatever was in it is on the planet now, unclassified, alive enough to register as organisms even if the system doesn't recognize them. If tap water works, other things might work too.

I look at the plant pot. The soil I just watered. Garden soil has things living in it, bacteria, fungi, microscopic worms, organisms that survive in conditions that aren't designed for them. The planet is cold and rocky, but organisms from cold climates survive cold climates. I don't know if that applies to minus 40. But I don't know that it doesn't.

I fill my palm with soil from the pot, wet enough to hold together, and press it against the planet panel.

Nothing. No confirmation. The panel doesn't react differently than it did with the water.

I check the planet.

---

[Planet designation: Sphere 7-A]

[Terrain: Rocky. Surface water detected in low-lying regions. Trace organic material detected. Origin: unclassified.]

[Unclassified organisms: Multiple variants detected. Status variable.]

[Life Energy generated: 0]

---

Multiple variants. The soil had more in it than the tap water.

0 Life Energy is still 0. But multiple variants means different organisms, different survival rates, different reproduction cycles. If one type survives and reproduces, the system has to recognize it eventually.

I wash my hands and go back to the application list.

The interview I'm most likely to hear back from is a receptionist position at a property management company that I applied to on day one. The posting is still up, which means they haven't hired anyone. I find the listing and read it again. They want someone who can handle a high volume of enquiries and stay organized under pressure. That's the job I just left, more or less. I write a follow-up email, short, name and application date at the top, one sentence about the role, a line about still being interested. I send it.

I write three more follow-up emails using the same format. Then I add five new applications.

By 11 PM I'm looking at nothing and I should sleep. The ball of light is in the corner of the room, a little brighter than it's been the last two days. I don't know if that means anything.

I check the planet one more time before I turn the lights off.

---

[Unclassified organisms: Reproduction event recorded. First generation descendants detected.]

[Life Energy generated: 0.003]

---

0.003.

It's not nothing.

I turn the lights off.

***

Three days later I get a call from the property management company. The woman on the phone is brisk and doesn't waste time. Can I come in Friday? I can. She gives me the address and hangs up.

I sit with that for a minute. Then I open the planet panel.

---

[Planet designation: Sphere 7-A]

[Status: Barren]

[Day 7 of 12: Water processing in progress.]

[Atmospheric development: 21% complete.]

[Purchased organisms (x40): 14 surviving.]

[Unclassified organisms: 3 variants active. Population expanding in low-lying water regions. Reproduction rate increasing.]

[Life Energy generated: 0.41]

---

0.41 from the unclassified organisms. The 14 surviving purchased organisms are generating almost nothing. The things from my plant pot are generating 0.41 Life Energy on a planet that is still minus 40 degrees, has no real atmosphere, and has been seeded for 7 days.

The atmosphere hits its minimum development threshold in 5 more days. Rent is due in 7 days. The property company interview is in 2 days. None of these numbers line up.

I write the interview details in my application list. Property company, receptionist, Friday. Under status I write: needs to go well.

Then I look at the plant pot. Still some soil left. Still some tap water in the building.

I get another glass.
